text: from the Latin, ratio, and therefore etymologically resides with notions that may seem quite far from current usage, Liminality and the Modern 100 Greek philosophy was developed very much as a defence against the overwhelming powers of imitation that the Sophists purposefully perpetuated within his city. This concern prompted Plato to suggest a need for limits on mimetic endeavours – rules that could prevent or tame inessential ‘craft-making’. While this attempt to exclude uncontrolled mimetic practices and influences often has caused modern commentators to label Plato ‘anti-democratic’ and ‘totalitarian’ (apparently wishing for an elitist thought police led by philosopher-Kings like himself), it should rather be recognized that Plato was indeed putting his finger on a crucial problem, and one that is as pressing in today’s ‘democratic order’ as it was then. However, in modern philosophy, starting from Descartes, this crucial – and necessary – focus on imitation and imitative behaviour largely disappeared. Or rather, it was hidden away. The view slowly became established that only animals and children imitate, whilst mature adults, in the Kantian spirit, reach maturity and autonomy – the ‘modern self’, this unquestioned premise onto which the main branches of the social and political sciences soon started to build their string of axioms. The sad consequence is that even today, no introductory textbook in sociology lists imitation among the ‘key concepts’. The emphasis on imitation returned to modern philosophy via a series of figures who are only now starting to receive the attention they deserve, and which are rarely discussed together. In this context, one must recognize Søren Kierkegaard as a relatively unrecognized background figure of the social sciences. Repetition (gjentagelsen) was one of his key interests, to which he returned again and again. Imitation is, of course, nothing but a special case of repetition. Repetition, for Kierkegaard, is potentially a deeply meaningful act – although the specific meaning of the repetition depends on the context, and on its acting out, how it moves the soul, and how the soul engages with it. Kierkegaard wrote Gjentagelsen in 1843, with the subtitle, Et Forsøg i den experimenterende Psychologie. 3 The writing coincided with his four visits to Berlin between 1841 and 1846, during the very same period that Benjamin later characterized as the ‘great firsts’ of the modern (see Katz 1998: 1): the city was going through radical transformations set in motion by the forces of industrialization, gas-lighting, train-travel and photography. It cannot be a coincidence that Gjentagelsen is the one essay in which Kierkegaard describes a visit to Berlin; he does it by narrating his own attempt to re-live a pleasant episode of his previous visit, tragically failing to walk in his own foot-steps. Kierkegaard, in this same writing, also singled out the technique of photographing as an imitative act, also here anticipating Benjamin. Within sociology, no-one, of course, has analysed imitation better than Gabriel Tarde. Tarde argued that the tendency towards imitation is the single most fundamental drive behind the creation and development of social institutions namely as having to do with something or some act that has the right proportions, or is in balance or harmony. Strictly speaking, much of modern philosophical rationalism is not rational. 3 Which translates as ‘An experiment in experimental psychology’. Dimensions of Liminality 101 (1903). Durkheim ridiculed Tarde for this idea outright, but luckily it is now easy to see who was right and wrong. In his preface to the second edition of Laws of Imitation, Tarde sought to answer the critiques that had been raised in response to his first edition, namely that he was ‘overstretching’ the term, and that his focus on imitation led him towards ‘psychology’ and extreme individualism: But I have always given it [imitation] a very precise and characteristic meaning, that of the action at a distance of one mind upon the other, and of action that consists of a quasi-photographic reproduction of a cerebral image upon the sensitive plate of another brain. [...] By imitation I mean, every impression of an inter-psychical photography, so to speak, willed or not willed, passive or active. (Tarde 1903: xiv) These Tardean and Kierkegaardian themes 4 were also key concerns for Walter Benjamin. Besides his Small History of Photography, there are references to photography throughout his works. Much of his perhaps most famous essay, The Work of Art in an Age of Mechanical Reproduction, is devoted to film and photography. As Benjamin sees it, photography captures the very essence of the age of mechanical reproduction. In 1933, while still in Berlin, Benjamin worked on two essays, on the Doctrine of the Similar (2005a) and On the Mimetic Faculty (2005b). While Benjamin’s thought often lapsed into mysticism (and Marxism), he clearly perceived that from within Kantianism and Enlightenment philosophy, the very nature of mimesis cannot even be posed as a problem. And he somehow also understood that he lived in a dangerously mimetic age. It is therefore also no coincidence that the very same period was also the one in which Kierkegaard was receiving a peak of attention among German social thinkers. Adorno wrote his dissertation while Nazism was on the march. It was therefore important for him to lift the interpretation of Kierkegaard out of Heidegger’s analytical embrace. His dissertation was, symbolically, published the very same day that Hitler rose to power. Also in 1933, Karl Löwith (1897–1973), wrote two central essays on Kierkegaard and Nietzsche. The reading of Kierkegaard guided his diagnostic attempt to capture and come to terms with the present and overcome nihilism (he was forced to flee Germany that same year), and most clearly so in the essay, Kierkegaard und Nietzsche oder Philosophische und Teologische Uberwindung des Nihilismus. None of those themes were alien to Arnold van Gennep. Van Gennep never referred to the authors here mentioned (with the evident exception of Plato). However, his life-long ethnographic interest in coins and ‘marking’ or stamping techniques clearly relates to the issue.
